title: Basic Nursing Care — Nutrition, Feeding & Therapeutic Diets (Set 5)
subtitle: Dysphagia & feeding technique, therapeutic diets, hydration, tube feeding & food-drug safety
scenario: Supporting nutrition and hydration: safe feeding technique, therapeutic diets, tube-feeding basics, and food-medication safety.

steps.q: A client with dysphagia is often ordered thickened liquids because:

opts.text: Thin liquids are harder to control and increase aspiration risk
opts.rat: Thin liquids move quickly and are difficult to control with dysphagia; thickening them slows flow and reduces aspiration risk.

opts.text: Thickened liquids taste better to everyone
opts.rat: The reason is safety (aspiration prevention), not taste.

opts.text: Thin liquids are always safest in dysphagia
opts.rat: Thin liquids are typically the highest aspiration risk in dysphagia.

opts.text: It has nothing to do with swallowing
opts.rat: Thickening is specifically a swallowing-safety measure.

steps.q: When feeding a client at risk for aspiration, the technician should:

opts.text: Feed the client quickly while lying flat
opts.rat: Lying flat and rushing greatly increase aspiration risk.

opts.text: Position upright, give small bites, and avoid rushing
opts.rat: Sitting the client upright, offering small bites/sips, allowing time, and minimizing talking while eating reduce aspiration risk.

opts.text: Give large mouthfuls to finish faster
opts.rat: Large bites increase choking and aspiration risk.

opts.text: Encourage talking and laughing during bites
opts.rat: Talking while swallowing increases aspiration risk.

steps.q: When assisting a client who needs help eating, the technician should:

opts.text: Rush the client to finish for the next task
opts.rat: Rushing risks choking and reduces intake and dignity.

opts.text: Feed the next bite before the client has swallowed
opts.rat: Giving the next bite too soon risks choking/aspiration.

opts.text: Allow adequate time, offer choices, and respect the client's pace
opts.rat: Patient, unhurried feeding with choices and respect for the client's pace supports intake and dignity.

opts.text: Force-feed if the client is slow
opts.rat: Force-feeding is unsafe and violates the client's rights.

steps.q: An order for 'NPO' means the client should:

opts.text: Eat a normal diet
opts.rat: NPO is the opposite of a normal diet.

opts.text: Drink extra fluids
opts.rat: NPO means no fluids by mouth.

opts.text: Eat only dessert
opts.rat: NPO permits nothing by mouth.

opts.text: Have nothing by mouth (no food or fluids)
opts.rat: NPO (nil per os) means nothing by mouth—no food or fluids—until the order is changed.

steps.q: Which item is allowed on a CLEAR liquid diet?

opts.text: Apple juice, broth, and plain gelatin
opts.rat: A clear liquid diet includes see-through liquids such as clear juices, broth, plain gelatin, and tea.

opts.text: A cheeseburger
opts.rat: Solid food is not part of a clear liquid diet.

opts.text: Whole milk
opts.rat: Milk is opaque and belongs to a full liquid diet, not clear liquids.

opts.text: Mashed potatoes
opts.rat: This is a solid/pureed food, not a clear liquid.

steps.q: A consistent-carbohydrate diet is used primarily for clients with:

opts.text: A broken arm
opts.rat: This diet is not indicated for fractures.

opts.text: Diabetes, to help manage blood glucose
opts.rat: A consistent-carbohydrate diet keeps carbohydrate intake steady across meals to help manage blood glucose in diabetes.

opts.text: Normal healthy adults with no condition
opts.rat: It is a therapeutic diet for glucose management, not a general diet.

opts.text: A simple cold
opts.rat: It is not prescribed for a cold.

steps.q: A client on a low-sodium diet should be taught to avoid:

opts.text: Fresh fruits and vegetables
opts.rat: Fresh produce is generally low in sodium and encouraged.

opts.text: Plain water
opts.rat: Water is not restricted for sodium content.

opts.text: Processed, canned, and cured foods and added table salt
opts.rat: Processed, canned, and cured foods are high in sodium; a low-sodium diet limits these and added salt, often for heart failure or hypertension.

opts.text: Home-cooked food without added salt
opts.rat: Unsalted home cooking is appropriate on a low-sodium diet.

steps.q: To help relieve and prevent constipation, a client is encouraged to eat a diet high in:

opts.text: Only refined white bread
opts.rat: Low-fiber refined foods contribute to constipation.

opts.text: No fiber at all
opts.rat: Removing fiber worsens constipation.

opts.text: Only cheese
opts.rat: A cheese-only diet is low in fiber and can worsen constipation.

opts.text: Fiber, along with adequate fluids
opts.rat: A high-fiber diet with adequate fluids and activity promotes normal bowel elimination and helps prevent constipation.

steps.q: The technician records how much a client ate at each meal because:

opts.text: Intake is monitored to track nutrition and identify problems early
opts.rat: Documenting the percentage/amount eaten helps the team monitor nutrition and catch declining intake early.

opts.text: It is unnecessary busywork
opts.rat: Intake documentation directly informs nutritional care.

opts.text: Only to fill out paperwork for fun
opts.rat: It serves a real clinical purpose.

opts.text: It should be guessed without looking
opts.rat: Intake should be observed and recorded accurately, not guessed.

steps.q: Which finding indicates a client is likely well hydrated?

opts.text: Very dark, scant urine
opts.rat: Dark, scant urine suggests dehydration, not good hydration.

opts.text: Pale-yellow urine and adequate urine output
opts.rat: Pale-yellow urine and adequate output suggest good hydration; dark, scant urine suggests dehydration.

opts.text: Dry, cracked lips and no urine for many hours
opts.rat: These are signs of dehydration.

opts.text: Sunken eyes and poor skin turgor
opts.rat: These indicate dehydration.

steps.q: When caring for a client receiving a tube (enteral) feeding, the technician should keep the head of the bed:

opts.text: Flat, with the client lying supine
opts.rat: Lying flat increases reflux and aspiration risk during tube feeding.

opts.text: In a head-down position
opts.rat: Head-down positioning increases aspiration risk.

opts.text: Elevated (at least about 30–45 degrees) during and after the feeding
opts.rat: Keeping the head of the bed elevated during and after tube feedings reduces reflux and aspiration risk.

opts.text: Turned face-down
opts.rat: Prone positioning is unsafe for tube feeding.

steps.q: A client's dietary restrictions include their cultural and religious food practices. The technician should:

opts.text: Ignore the preferences and serve anything
opts.rat: Disregarding these practices is disrespectful and may violate the client's beliefs.

opts.text: Tell the client their beliefs do not matter
opts.rat: This is disrespectful and non-therapeutic.

opts.text: Force the client to eat restricted foods
opts.rat: Forcing restricted foods violates the client's rights and beliefs.

opts.text: Respect and accommodate the client's cultural/religious food preferences
opts.rat: Honoring cultural and religious dietary practices respects the client and supports their intake and dignity.

steps.q: A significant unintended change in a client's weight should be:

opts.text: Reported, as it may signal a nutritional or medical problem
opts.rat: Notable weight loss or gain can indicate nutritional deficits, fluid shifts, or illness and should be reported for evaluation.

opts.text: Ignored unless the client complains
opts.rat: Weight changes are monitored proactively, not only on complaint.

opts.text: Kept secret from the nurse
opts.rat: Findings must be communicated to the team.

opts.text: Assumed to be unimportant
opts.rat: Weight changes can be clinically important.

steps.q: Which findings may indicate malnutrition in a client?

opts.text: Steady weight and good energy
opts.rat: These are signs of adequate nutrition.

opts.text: Unintended weight loss, muscle wasting, and poor wound healing
opts.rat: Weight loss, muscle wasting, fatigue, and poor wound healing are signs of malnutrition that warrant reporting.

opts.text: Rapid wound healing and strong muscles
opts.rat: These reflect good, not poor, nutrition.

opts.text: Excellent appetite with stable weight
opts.rat: This is reassuring, not a sign of malnutrition.

steps.q: A client taking warfarin (a blood thinner) is taught to keep intake of which nutrient CONSISTENT?

opts.text: Vitamin C from citrus only
opts.rat: Vitamin C is not the key interacting nutrient for warfarin.

opts.text: Table salt
opts.rat: Sodium is not the warfarin-interaction concern.

opts.text: Vitamin K (found in green leafy vegetables)
opts.rat: Vitamin K affects warfarin's action; clients keep vitamin K intake (leafy greens) consistent rather than suddenly increasing or decreasing it.

opts.text: Caffeine
opts.rat: Caffeine is not the vitamin that interacts with warfarin.

steps.q: Grapefruit juice is a concern with certain medications because it can:

opts.text: Have no effect on any medication ever
opts.rat: Grapefruit juice interacts with a number of medications.

opts.text: Cure the underlying illness
opts.rat: It does not treat the illness; it can cause interactions.

opts.text: Replace the need for medication
opts.rat: It does not replace medication.

opts.text: Alter how some medications are metabolized, changing their levels
opts.rat: Grapefruit juice can interfere with the metabolism of certain medications, raising drug levels and the risk of adverse effects; clients should check with the prescriber/pharmacist.

steps.q: When serving a meal to a client with visual impairment, a helpful technique is to:

opts.text: Describe the location of foods using a clock-face reference
opts.rat: Describing plate contents by clock positions (e.g., 'potatoes at 6 o'clock') helps a client with visual impairment eat independently.

opts.text: Say nothing and let the client guess
opts.rat: Leaving the client to guess reduces independence and intake.

opts.text: Remove the plate before they finish
opts.rat: Removing food early reduces intake and dignity.

opts.text: Feed them without asking
opts.rat: Assuming the client cannot self-feed removes autonomy; offer assistance based on need and preference.

steps.q: To encourage adequate fluid intake in a client at risk for dehydration, the technician should:

opts.text: Restrict all fluids
opts.rat: Restricting fluids worsens dehydration risk.

opts.text: Offer preferred fluids frequently and keep them within reach
opts.rat: Frequently offering preferred, accessible fluids helps maintain hydration, especially in older or cognitively impaired clients.

opts.text: Only offer fluids once per day
opts.rat: Infrequent offering is inadequate for hydration.

opts.text: Place fluids out of the client's reach
opts.rat: Out-of-reach fluids discourage intake.

steps.q: A client on a mechanically altered (pureed) diet is served this texture because they:

opts.text: Simply prefer baby food
opts.rat: The diet is prescribed for swallowing/chewing safety, not mere preference.

opts.text: Need extra crunchy foods
opts.rat: Crunchy foods would be unsafe for these clients.

opts.text: Have difficulty chewing or swallowing regular-texture food safely
opts.rat: Pureed/mechanically altered diets are ordered when a client cannot safely chew or swallow regular textures, reducing choking/aspiration risk.

opts.text: Are being punished
opts.rat: Diet textures are for safety, never punishment.

steps.q: To help prevent choking during meals, the technician should:

opts.text: Leave clients who are at risk to eat alone
opts.rat: At-risk clients need supervision during meals.

opts.text: Serve large chunks of tough food to at-risk clients
opts.rat: Large, tough pieces increase choking risk.

opts.text: Have clients eat quickly while lying down
opts.rat: Eating fast while reclined increases choking and aspiration risk.

opts.text: Supervise at-risk clients, ensure proper positioning, and watch for distress
opts.rat: Supervising at-risk clients, positioning them upright, providing appropriate food textures, and watching for signs of distress help prevent choking.

reference: Aligned to the California BVNPT Psychiatric Technician exam content area: Basic Nursing Care. Written from standard psychiatric-technician and nursing references. Pending faculty review.
